items.q: Is a top-mark performance simply one with no wrong notes?
items.correct: No - accuracy matters, but marks also reward interpretation, stylistic awareness, tone and communication with the listener.
items.misconception: A perfect performance just means playing all the right notes in the right order.
items.random_wrong: Marks are awarded mainly for how difficult the outfit and stage presentation are.
items.why: Assessment criteria for performing units weigh musical understanding and expression alongside accuracy; a note-perfect but expressionless performance loses marks on interpretation.

items.q: What is the best general strategy for practising a piece?
items.correct: Work in a targeted way: isolate difficult sections, practise them slowly, then reassemble the piece.
items.misconception: Practising means playing the piece straight through from the beginning every time.
items.random_wrong: Practising means listening to recordings instead of playing.
items.why: Whole run-throughs over-rehearse the easy opening and under-rehearse the hard spots; targeted section work fixes errors before they become ingrained habits.

items.q: Are 'intonation' and 'tone quality' the same thing?
items.correct: No - intonation is the accuracy of tuning, while tone quality describes the timbre or sound colour you produce.
items.misconception: Intonation just means having a nice sound.
items.random_wrong: Intonation means the volume level of the performance.
items.why: A player can produce a beautiful tone that is consistently sharp: the two skills are assessed separately, so students need precise vocabulary for each.

items.q: What is the most effective way to fix a fast passage that keeps going wrong?
items.correct: Practise it slowly and increase the tempo gradually
items.misconception: Play it repeatedly at full speed
items.random_wrong: Leave it out and hope for the best
items.why: Correct answer: Practise it slowly and increase the tempo gradually. “Play it repeatedly at full speed” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The accuracy of tuning of the pitches you produce
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The accuracy of tuning of the pitches you produce. “The loudness of your playing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Stylistic awareness in a performance means what?
items.correct: Applying the conventions of the piece's period and genre
items.misconception: Playing every piece in the same way
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Applying the conventions of the piece's period and genre. “Playing every piece in the same way” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If you make a small slip during a performance, the best response is to do what?
items.correct: Keep going and maintain the pulse
items.why: It's Keep going and maintain the pulse. “Stop and restart the piece” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
